description:
You are a capacity-planner who has just finished a week-long study of a small two-node web cluster.  
    Management now wants two deliverables:  

    1.  A “planned” firewall script that the operations team can pipe straight into their automation system.  
    2.  A compact comma-separated log that captures the *peak* resource-utilisation samples on which you based your plan.  

    Create all files inside the directory
        /home/user/security/firewall
    (create the directory tree if it does not yet exist).

    --------------------------------------------------------------------
    DETAILS THE SCRIPT **MUST** IMPLEMENT
    --------------------------------------------------------------------
    • The script file name must be exactly: planned_rules_v1.sh  
    • It must be **executable** ( `chmod 755` ) and start with a she-bang on the very first line:
          #!/bin/bash

    • The body of the script has to contain *exactly* the following
      iptables commands, in the same order and with no extra spacing:  

          iptables -F
          iptables -P INPUT DROP
          iptables -P FORWARD DROP
          iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
          iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
          iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
          iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
          iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
          iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
          iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 10.0.0.0/24 --dport 9090 -j ACCEPT
          iptables -A INPUT -j LOG --log-prefix "IPTables-Dropped: "
          iptables -A INPUT -j DROP

    • Add one blank line after the final command.  
    • No other lines, comments, or whitespace are permitted in the script (the automated test is strict).

    --------------------------------------------------------------------
    DETAILS THE LOG FILE **MUST** FOLLOW
    --------------------------------------------------------------------
    • Log file name:  resource_capacity.log  
    • Place it in the same directory as the script.  

    • The file is CSV with **exactly six lines**:
        – 1 header line  
        – 5 data lines  

    • Header line (case-sensitive, no spaces):
          TIMESTAMP,CPU_USED_PCT,MEM_USED_PCT,NET_IN_KBPS,NET_OUT_KBPS

    • Each data line must follow the pattern:  
          YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ,<integer 0-100>,<integer 0-100>,<integer>,<integer>

      Example (do **NOT** copy): 2023-08-15T10:00:00Z,73,62,1250,980  

      Rules:
        – The timestamp must be in UTC and end with “Z”.  
        – CPU and MEM numbers are whole percentages.  
        – NET_IN_KBPS and NET_OUT_KBPS are positive integers ≤ 100000.  
        – Use a different timestamp for every line and keep them in ascending order.

    --------------------------------------------------------------------
    FINAL CHECKLIST
    --------------------------------------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/security/firewall/planned_rules_v1.sh  
       • Exact contents and ordering as specified.  
       • Executable bit set.

    2. /home/user/security/firewall/resource_capacity.log  
       • Six lines total, comma-separated, strict column order, no trailing whitespace.

    When you are finished, list the contents of /home/user/security/firewall so that the automated grader can see the file names and permissions.
truth:
<!--
        The test harness will verify:

        Directory:
        /home/user/security/firewall    (must exist)

        File #1: /home/user/security/firewall/planned_rules_v1.sh
        Permissions: 755
        Content EXACTLY (including blank final line):
            #!/bin/bash
            iptables -F
            iptables -P INPUT DROP
            iptables -P FORWARD DROP
            iptables -P OUTPUT ACCEPT
            iptables -A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT
            iptables -A INPUT -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
            iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
            iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
            iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
            iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 10.0.0.0/24 --dport 9090 -j ACCEPT
            iptables -A INPUT -j LOG --log-prefix "IPTables-Dropped: "
            iptables -A INPUT -j DROP

        (Note the single trailing newline after the last line.)

        File #2: /home/user/security/firewall/resource_capacity.log
        • First line exactly:
            TIMESTAMP,CPU_USED_PCT,MEM_USED_PCT,NET_IN_KBPS,NET_OUT_KBPS

        • Exactly 5 data lines follow, each matching regex:
            ^20[0-9]{2}-[0-1][0-9]-[0-3][0-9]T[0-2][0-9]:[0-5][0-9]:[0-5][0-9]Z,[0-9]{1,3},[0-9]{1,3},[0-9]+,[0-9]+$

        • Timestamps strictly ascending lexicographically.
        • CPU_USED_PCT & MEM_USED_PCT integers in 0-100 inclusive.
        • NET_IN_KBPS & NET_OUT_KBPS positive integers ≤ 100000.

        The grader will also `ls -l /home/user/security/firewall` and expect to
        see at least:

            -rwxr-xr-x 1 user user <size> planned_rules_v1.sh
            -rw-r--r-- 1 user user <size> resource_capacity.log
    -->
